title: Morphological traits of informality in Sub-Saharan African cities
markdown:
By [**Ana Maria Ricchiardi Hernandez**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ana-ricchiardi/), 2023

**_Topic:**_ Urbanisation 

**_Keywords:_** Mapping; Developing regions; Informal settlements; Remote sensing; Urban form, Satellite imagery, Geographic Information Systems, GIS

_**Africapolis data:**_ GIS, agglomeration data

_**Link to the research presentation**_: [Mapping Transitional Urban Forms From Afar] (https://strathprints.strath.ac.uk/80394/1/Ricchiardi_ISUF_2021_Mapping_transitional_urban_forms_from.pdf)

___

Undoubtedly urban development in the last decades has produced profound spatial changes at a global scale. These **spatial changes** have been seen with higher intensity in **Global South** contexts and in particular in the African continent. The reasons for such changes, among others, have to do with **demographic, economic and political transitions**.

###Morphological characters of informality

In morphological terms, cities in Sub-Saharan Africa present fragmented **morphological patterns**. These patterns include planned and unplanned areas. The **recognition of informal ways of creating urban spaces becomes crucial** when seeing cities in the Global South in ways that move away from western visions of the urban. Informal areas endure, grow and sit in the collective imagination of cities in the 21st century. A big part of the urban population in Sub-Saharan Africa lives in areas that fit the various definitions of urban informality.  

As **informality** has become in many places the **rule rather than the exception**, improving the knowledge of how unplanned areas develop should be considered an urgent matter in contemporary research on cities. The increasing **availability of morphological data** has improved the systematic **measurements of morphological indices of informal settlements**, but only few studies deal with the urban morphology of these settlements in the context of Sub-Saharan Africa, leaving space for observations to be made. 

In the last decades, open-source software, mobile phone applications, and volunteered geographic information have reshaped how scholars and practitioners interact with data about cities. Online satellite services like [_Google Earth_](https://earth.google.com/web/@39.20985322,9.18619606,12.46938184a,126975.86365929d,35y,0.00000012h,4.55724682t,0r), crowdsourced data sites like [_Open Street Map (OSM)_](https://www.openstreetmap.org/#map=6/46.449/2.210) and ongoing, long-term projects like [_Africapolis_](https://africapolis.org/), represent potential **tools to characterize and compare spatial trends and morphologies of recent changes in the continent**. 

###Methodology

Through sampling, this research work mapped case studies in the context of Sub-Saharan Africa, one for each major region, as defined by the African Union.

#####*Map 1. Localisation of the case studies across sub-Saharan Africa*
![img](/images/tempimages/Ricchiardi_1.jpg)

The study referred to spatial and statistical information coming from _Africapolis_ and other open-source datasets. A flexible understanding of urban forms without fixed boundaries but as units that illustrate urban growth phenomena is made possible with the information provided by _Africapolis_. Defining **urban agglomerations** as **units whose shapes, contents and boundaries change over time** as a result of evolving built environments shows how in many cases, municipal and **political boundaries cannot keep pace with the growth of urban forms**. 


For this work, information on the **most populated metropole agglomerations** in each region was **interpolated with [UN data on slum dwellers](https://data.unhabitat.org/pages/housing-slums-and-informal-settlements).** The use of mixed methodologies allowed the exemplification of the informal phenomena through four case studies. The study employed **comparative, diachronic reasoning** for the samples and an **analytical approach and deductive observation** of the open-sourced material obtained. Through visual image interpretation, a manual mapping method for the collection of morphological data, the mapping operation highlighted recurrences of spatial patterns in informal developments.  

#####*Map 2. Morphological mapping of samples in Luanda, Lagos, Kinshasa and Nairobi*
![img](/images/tempimages/Ricchiardi_2.jpg)

The observation of the samples resulted in the definition of two types of morphological permutations. These permutations occur through incremental additive and subtractive (or) substitutive processes of change.  

* _Transformative morphological permutation_ refers to **radical alterations or changes in outlook, character, form and condition** in the observed morphologies. These types of modifications in the mapped samples relate to situations that deal with **urban regeneration projects** and that involve the clearance and/or removal of informal morphologies in the areas of intervention.

* _Adaptive morphological permutation_ **refers to modifications that appear to deal with a change in the condition of the place where the morphology develops**. These modifications deal with **small demolitions and incremental additions to existing morphologies** through a process of room-by-room growth. 

Empirical urban and social studies suggest that the processes of modification of such morphologies have intrinsic links to tangible and intangible situational aspects of the settlements. The contextual aspects deal primarily (but not exclusively) with urban regulations, access to services, space for growth, the possibility of creating capital and the cost of land.  

The acknowledgement of morphological traits in these samples forces another recognition: the recognition that **informality in the urban works as a way of spatial production with tangible and recognizable traits in constant transformation**. 

*The views, conclusions and materials of external contributors do not represent the views of OECD/SWAC or its Members.*
___
###*Author’s biography* 

[**Ana Maria Ricchiardi Hernandez**] (https://www.linkedin.com/in/ana-ricchiardi/) is an Ecuadorian architect based in Turin, Italy. She has carried out professional, academic and training activities in Ecuador and Italy. Ricchiardi’s work focuses on urban scenarios of Global South contexts. 

From the same author: ["Transitional Morphologies and Urban Forms: Generation and Regeneration Processes—An Agenda" (2021)](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/13/11/6233) and  ["Co-evolving morphologies in Sub-Saharan Africa" (2020)](https://iris.polito.it/retrieve/handle/11583/2873896/435403)
